blocks.body: Algebraic properties are rules that hold true for all numbers. Understanding them lets you rearrange and simplify expressions confidently.
Commutative Property of Addition: a + b = b + a. The order of addition doesn't matter. Example: 3 + 5 = 5 + 3 = 8.
Commutative Property of Multiplication: a × b = b × a. The order of multiplication doesn't matter. Example: 4 × 7 = 7 × 4 = 28.
"To commute" means to move around. The Commutative Properties work only for addition and multiplication — NOT for subtraction or division. 5 - 3 ≠ 3 - 5.
Associative Property of Addition: (a + b) + c = a + (b + c). How you group numbers when adding doesn't change the sum. Example: (1 + 2) + 5 = 1 + (2 + 5) = 8.
Associative Property of Multiplication: (a × b) × c = a × (b × c). How you group numbers when multiplying doesn't change the product. Example: (2 × 3) × 5 = 2 × (3 × 5) = 30.
Commutative = changing the ORDER. Associative = changing the GROUPING. Both work only for addition and multiplication.

Distributive Property: a(b + c) = a×b + a×c. Multiplying a number by a sum equals the sum of multiplying by each addend separately. Example: 3(4 + 5) = 3×4 + 3×5 = 12 + 15 = 27. Also works with subtraction: a(b - c) = ab - ac.
Identity Property of Addition: a + 0 = a. Adding 0 to any number leaves it unchanged. 0 is the additive identity. Example: 7 + 0 = 7.
Identity Property of Multiplication: a × 1 = a. Multiplying any number by 1 leaves it unchanged. 1 is the multiplicative identity. Example: 9 × 1 = 9.
Inverse Property of Addition: a + (-a) = 0. Every number has an opposite (additive inverse) that sums to zero. Example: 5 + (-5) = 0.
Inverse Property of Multiplication: a × (1/a) = 1, for a ≠ 0. Every non-zero number has a reciprocal that multiplies to 1. Example: 3 × (1/3) = 1.
